county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Taylorville holds records from 1839. Created from Sangamon, Montgomery, and Shelby — check all three parent counties for earlier records. Coal mining became important in the late 1800s; mine employment records at Illinois State Archives document worker families. Christian County was in Lincoln's Eighth Judicial Circuit — court records reflect his practice. Christian County Historical Society in Taylorville. IRAD at University of Illinois Springfield. Strong Methodist and Christian Church (Disciples) records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1839. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1839. County Clerk issues licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (details improve post-1870s). Statewide marriage registration from 1962.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1839. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1839. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Christian County
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: BLM-GLO digitized — searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Illinois State Archives / Illinois Department of Mines and Minerals
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mine inspection reports, employment records, accident reports, and company records. Illinois coal mining drew workers from Italy, Lithuania, Poland, and other European countries as well as Appalachian migrants. Annual coal reports published by the State of Illinois list mines and operators.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mine inspection records at Illinois State Archives; some company records in local collections

source_profiles.source_name: Eighth Judicial Circuit Court Records — Christian County
source_profiles.repository: Christian County Circuit Clerk, Taylorville / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Abraham Lincoln practiced law on Illinois' Eighth Judicial Circuit from 1837 to 1860. Circuit court records from this period include cases Lincoln argued and contemporaries he worked with. Court records also document the legal, economic, and social history of central Illinois families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Circuit court records at county level; Lincoln Legal Papers project has compiled many Lincoln-related documents

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Marshall holds records from 1819. One of Illinois' early counties — early settlers came primarily from Kentucky, Virginia, and North Carolina via the Upland South migration pattern. Clark County was on the old Vincennes-to-St. Louis trace — early road and tavern records. Marshall is one of the smallest county seats in Illinois. Check Crawford County for pre-1819 records. Clark County Historical Society. IRAD at Eastern Illinois University in Charleston. Oil discovery in the early 1900s brought new land records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1819. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1819. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1819. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax records at Illinois State Archives on microfilm; IRAD holdings vary by county

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Louisville holds records from 1824. Created from Wayne, Crawford, and Fayette counties — check all three for earlier records. Clay County is in the Little Wabash River valley; early settlers were predominantly Upland South migrants. Flora (though in Clay County) became an important railroad town. Oil boom in early 1900s generated extensive lease and land transaction records. Clay County Genealogical Society. IRAD at Eastern Illinois University. Illinois State Archives has early land patents.

source_profiles.source_name: Oil and Gas Lease Records — Clay County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oil and gas lease agreements recorded in county deed books. These records identify landowners and track property transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved in county deed records

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Carlyle holds records from 1824. Carlyle was a major German settlement center — Looking Glass Prairie attracted Germans from 1830s onward. German Catholic parish records (St. Mary's, Carlyle) are essential. Carlyle was the second state capital of Illinois (before Vandalia). Clinton County Historical Society. Strong German Evangelical and Lutheran church records. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Edwardsville. Check Washington and Bond counties for pre-1824 records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1824. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.repository: Clinton County Circuit Clerk, Carlyle / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court. Post-1906 copies at NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago). Illinois attracted massive European immigration — German, Irish, Swedish, Norwegian, Czech, Polish, Italian, Lithuanian, and other groups.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Illinois naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry

source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Belleville / Diocese of Springfield / local parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German Catholic and Lutheran parish records — baptisms, marriages, and burials. Many parishes kept records in Latin or German. Parish registers often include parents' birthplaces in Germany. German settlement in Illinois was extensive, particularly in southwestern and central Illinois.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish records generally well-preserved; some microfilmed by FamilySearch

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Charleston holds records from 1830. Abraham Lincoln's father Thomas Lincoln settled in Coles County in 1831 — Lincoln Log Cabin State Historic Site. Eastern Illinois University (1895) in Charleston holds IRAD collections for east-central Illinois counties. Coles County Historical Society. Lincoln-Douglas debate site in Charleston (1858). Check Clark and Edgar counties for pre-1830 records. Strong Upland South settlement heritage from Kentucky and Tennessee. Methodist and Baptist church records are important.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1871 Great Chicago Fire destroyed Cook County courthouse and most county records. Pre-1871 deeds, court records, probate, and vital records largely lost. Some records reconstructed from private papers and duplicate filings.
county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Chicago. CRITICAL: The 1871 Great Chicago Fire destroyed the Cook County courthouse and most pre-1871 county records including deeds, court files, and vital records. For pre-1871 Cook County research, use: Newberry Library (premier Midwest genealogy collection), Chicago History Museum, Archdiocese of Chicago parish registers, and city directories. Post-1871 records rebuilt. Cook County is the largest county in Illinois by population. Chicago was a massive immigration hub — Polish, German, Irish, Italian, Czech, Lithuanian, Swedish, Greek, Jewish communities created extensive ethnic church, fraternal, and newspaper records. Great Migration brought enormous African American population growth 1910-1970. Circuit Court Archives has records from 1871 forward.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1831. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1871 Great Chicago Fire destroyed most pre-1871 deed records; surviving records reconstructed from private copies and duplicate filings

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1831. County Clerk issues licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (details improve post-1870s). Statewide marriage registration from 1962.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1871 marriage records largely destroyed in Great Chicago Fire; post-1871 records intact

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1831. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1871 probate records largely destroyed in Great Chicago Fire; post-1871 records intact

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1831. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1871 court records destroyed in Great Chicago Fire; Circuit Court Archives holds post-1871 records

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Cook County
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: BLM-GLO digitized — searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Cook County Circuit Clerk, Chicago / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court. Post-1906 copies at NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago). Illinois attracted massive European immigration — German, Irish, Swedish, Norwegian, Czech, Polish, Italian, Lithuanian, and other groups.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Illinois naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry

source_profiles.source_name: African American Church and Community Records — Cook County
source_profiles.repository: Vivian G. Harsh Research Collection (Chicago Public Library) / local churches and historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: African American church records (AME, Baptist, COGIC), fraternal organization records, and community newspapers. The Great Migration (1910-1970) brought hundreds of thousands of African Americans from the South to Illinois, particularly Chicago and industrial cities. Chicago Defender newspaper is a critical source.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Vivian G. Harsh Collection at Chicago Public Library is the largest African American history collection in the Midwest

source_profiles.source_name: Illinois & Michigan Canal Records — Cook County
source_profiles.repository: Illinois State Archives / I&M Canal National Heritage Corridor
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Canal construction records, land sales, and worker rolls. The I&M Canal (1836-1848) connected Lake Michigan to the Illinois River. Canal construction employed thousands of Irish immigrant laborers. Canal land grant records document property sales along the corridor.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Canal Commission records at Illinois State Archives

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Robinson holds records from 1816, making Crawford one of the earliest Illinois counties. Crawford County was carved from Edwards County and includes some of the oldest American settlement in eastern Illinois. Oil discovery in the early 1900s at the Robinson pool brought significant economic activity and land records. Crawford County Historical Society. IRAD at Eastern Illinois University. Check Edwards County for pre-1816 records. Early settlers came predominantly from Kentucky and Virginia. Heath candy bar was invented in Robinson.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1816. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Toledo holds records from 1843. Check Coles County for pre-1843 records. Cumberland County is one of Illinois' smallest and most rural counties. Early settlers came from Kentucky, Tennessee, and southern Indiana — Upland South migration. Cumberland County Historical and Genealogical Society. Embarras River communities. IRAD at Eastern Illinois University. Township records and school district records supplement sparse county records. Strong Baptist and Methodist church records from early settlement.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Clinton holds records from 1839. De Witt County is in the heart of central Illinois prairie. Abraham Lincoln practiced law here on the Eighth Judicial Circuit. Clinton was named for DeWitt Clinton of New York (Erie Canal fame). Illinois Central Railroad divided the county. Check McLean and Macon counties for pre-1839 records. De Witt County Genealogical Society. IRAD at Illinois State University. Strong Methodist and Presbyterian church records. Weldon Springs area — early settlement records.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Sycamore holds records from 1837. DeKalb County is the birthplace of barbed wire — Joseph Glidden's invention (1874) in DeKalb transformed prairie farming. Northern Illinois University (1895) in DeKalb holds IRAD collections for northeastern Illinois. DeKalb County History Center. Scandinavian and German settlers in the 1840s-1850s. Check Kane County for pre-1837 records. Strong agricultural heritage — Ellwood House Museum documents Glidden family and barbed wire era. Railroad towns along the Chicago & Northwestern.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1837. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: Swedish American Church Records — DeKalb County
source_profiles.repository: Swenson Swedish Immigration Research Center, Rock Island / Augustana Heritage Association
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Augustana Lutheran and other Swedish denomination church records — baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials. Bishop Hill Colony records (Henry County). Swenson Center at Augustana College holds the largest Swedish American research collection in the U.S.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Swenson Center has extensive microfilm and original records; many indexed
